edges.description: Loss of catalytic enzyme function reduces urea production and permits ammonia accumulation.
edges.target: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: ASL loss impairs systemic nitric oxide production through reduced endogenous arginine synthesis and impaired use of extracellular arginine.
intermediate_mechanisms: impaired extracellular arginine utilization for nitric oxide production
edges.target: Nitric oxide deficiency in ASL deficiency

edges.description: ASL deficiency produces a subtype-specific hepatic redox branch with glutathione depletion and altered cysteine utilization.
edges.target: Hepatic glutathione dysregulation in ASL deficiency

edges.description: ARG1 loss creates the distal urea-cycle branch dominated by hyperargininemia and progressive spastic neurologic disease.
edges.target: Arginine and guanidino compound neurotoxicity in ARG1 deficiency

edges.description: Ammonia excess drives astrocyte glutamine loading, edema, and encephalopathic brain injury.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier and is converted to glutamine in astrocytes.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: Catabolic illness, fasting, surgery, or protein loading can overwhelm residual urea-cycle flux and precipitate acute decompensation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Increased endogenous protein catabolism raises nitrogen production.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation
edges.target: Catabolic nitrogen stress exceeding residual ureagenesis

edges.description: In proximal defects, excess carbamoyl phosphate is diverted toward pyrimidine synthesis and orotate production.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation
edges.target: Carbamoyl phosphate overflow to pyrimidine pathway

edges.description: Failed hepatic nitrogen disposal manifests clinically as hyperammonemia.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: Reduced ammonia-to-urea conversion raises circulating ammonia.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: Excess nitrogen is shunted into glutamine, producing hyperglutaminemia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ammonia is incorporated into glutamine by glutamine synthetase.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: The position of the enzymatic block produces subtype-specific citrulline abnormalities, low in proximal defects and high in ASS1 deficiency.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: ASL deficiency blocks argininosuccinate cleavage and causes argininosuccinic acid accumulation.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: UCDs can develop chronic liver disease through incompletely resolved mechanisms downstream of the inherited urea-cycle defect.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

edges.description: Excess nitrogen production during catabolism precipitates acute ammonia elevation when residual ureagenesis is insufficient.
intermediate_mechanisms: Increased proteolysis raises ammonia substrate load.
edges.source: Catabolic nitrogen stress exceeding residual ureagenesis

edges.description: Acute decompensation in late-onset UCDs commonly includes nausea and vomiting.
edges.source: Catabolic nitrogen stress exceeding residual ureagenesis

edges.description: Hyperammonemic decompensation can present with hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis.
edges.source: Catabolic nitrogen stress exceeding residual ureagenesis

edges.description: Astrocyte glutamine accumulation acts as an osmolyte and increases brain volume.
intermediate_mechanisms: Astrocytic glutamine synthetase converts ammonia and glutamate into glutamine.

edges.description: Ammonia-mediated neurotransmission toxicity and astrocyte swelling produce metabolic encephalopathy.
Altered alertness is an early neurologic manifestation of ammonia toxicity.
Ammonia and glutamine elevations increase seizure risk during hyperammonemic crises.
Severe intracranial hypertension and metabolic encephalopathy can progress to coma and death.
Survivors of neonatal or recurrent hyperammonemic injury can develop long-term cognitive impairment.
Recurrent or neonatal ammonia-mediated brain injury impairs neurodevelopment.
Hyperammonemia and toxic metabolite accumulation can produce increased neuromuscular tone, spasticity, and clonus.
Proximal carbamoyl phosphate excess is diverted to pyrimidine synthesis and appears diagnostically as elevated urinary orotic acid, especially in OTC deficiency.
edges.source: Carbamoyl phosphate overflow to pyrimidine pathway

edges.description: Hepatomegaly is one reported manifestation within the chronic liver disease spectrum of UCDs.
ASL-associated glutathione depletion and antioxidant-pathway dysregulation contribute to the chronic liver-disease branch of argininosuccinic aciduria.
edges.source: Hepatic glutathione dysregulation in ASL deficiency

edges.description: NO-mediated central catecholamine dysregulation is associated with the late-onset movement-disorder phenotype in ASA.
intermediate_mechanisms: NO-mediated downregulation of central catecholamine biosynthesis
edges.source: Nitric oxide deficiency in ASL deficiency

edges.description: Developmental delay occurs within the ASA neurological phenotype associated with NO-mediated central catecholamine dysregulation.
intermediate_mechanisms: NO-mediated downregulation of central catecholamine biosynthesis

edges.description: Epilepsy occurs within the ASA neurological phenotype associated with NO-mediated central catecholamine dysregulation.
intermediate_mechanisms: NO-mediated downregulation of central catecholamine biosynthesis

edges.description: Loss of ARG1-mediated arginine hydrolysis produces persistent hyperargininemia.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia and toxic metabolite accumulation can produce increased neuromuscular tone, spasticity, and ankle clonus during severe decompensation.

evidence.reference_title: Citrullinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: Without prompt intervention, hyperammonemia and the accumulation of other toxic metabolites (e.g., glutamine) result in increased ICP, increased neuromuscular tone, spasticity, ankle clonus, seizures, loss of consciousness, and death.

nodes.description: Emergency management includes cessation of protein intake, high-calorie glucose infusion to reverse catabolism, intravenous nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate), and arginine supplementation. When ammonia exceeds 500 umol/L or encephalopathy is present, extracorporeal clearance with continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) is recommended.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: The strategy for therapy is to prevent the irreversible toxicity of high-ammonia exposure to the brain.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: arginase 1) are present in the cytoplasm

nodes.description: Arginine supplementation replaces the amino acid that cannot be synthesized endogenously in most UCDs (except ARG1 deficiency). Citrulline supplementation may be preferable as it bypasses intestinal and hepatic first-pass metabolism and has been associated with lower mean ammonia concentrations compared to arginine alone.

evidence.reference_title: The nutritional management of urea cycle disorders.
evidence.snippet: Most patients, except those with arginase deficiency, will need supplements of arginine

nodes.description: ARG1 deficiency is a distal urea-cycle defect in which chronic hyperargininemia and related guanidino compound accumulation, rather than recurrent severe hyperammonemia alone, dominate the progressive neurologic phenotype. This subtype-specific toxic-metabolite mechanism explains spastic paraplegia-predominant disease.
nodes.id: Arginine and guanidino compound neurotoxicity in ARG1 deficiency

evidence.reference_title: Arginase-1 deficiency.
evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: argininosuccinate synthetase, argininosuccinate lyase and arginase 1) are present in the cytoplasm

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: argininosuccinate synthetase, argininosuccinate lyase and arginase 1) are present in the cytoplasm

nodes.description: In proximal urea cycle defects, particularly OTC deficiency, carbamoyl phosphate accumulates in the mitochondrial matrix and is diverted into the cytosolic pyrimidine synthesis pathway, leading to elevated urinary orotic acid. This overflow is diagnostically useful for distinguishing OTC deficiency from CPS1 deficiency.
nodes.id: Carbamoyl phosphate overflow to pyrimidine pathway

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: two of which, carbamoylphosphate synthetase 1 and ornithine transcarbamylase are present in the mitochondrial matrix

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: carbamoylphosphate synthetase 1 and ornithine transcarbamylase are present in the mitochondrial matrix

nodes.description: Carglumic acid (Carbaglu) is a synthetic analog of N-acetylglutamate that directly activates CPS1. It is specifically indicated for NAGS deficiency and is also used for hyperammonemia in organic acidemias. It provides pharmacological bypass of the NAGS enzyme deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: N-acetylglutamate synthase and at least two transporter proteins are essential to urea cycle function.

nodes.description: Catabolic stressors such as infection, fasting, surgery, childbirth, or high protein intake increase endogenous protein breakdown and nitrogen load. In patients with partial residual urea-cycle function, this excess nitrogen can exceed detoxification capacity and precipitate acute hyperammonemic crises.
nodes.id: Catabolic nitrogen stress exceeding residual ureagenesis

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders in adult patients: a tightrope walk between evidence-based medicine and expert opinion-a case series and systematic review.
evidence.snippet: During metabolic stress induced by infection, childbirth or surgery, a catabolic phase leads to the metabolism of a large amount of proteins. This excess intake will exceed the capacity of the urea cycle, especially if it has an enzymatic deficit. A significant production of ammonia then follows.

nodes.description: Cytotoxic cerebral edema driven by astrocytic glutamine accumulation and osmotic swelling is a life-threatening complication of severe hyperammonemic crises, contributing to raised intracranial pressure.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: The strategy for therapy is to prevent the irreversible toxicity of high-ammonia exposure to the brain.

nodes.description: Children and adults with UCDs can develop chronic liver disease even outside acute hyperammonemic episodes. Reported manifestations include abnormal liver enzymes, hepatomegaly, abnormal liver ultrasound, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis with portal hypertension, impaired liver function, and liver failure requiring transplantation.

evidence.reference_title: Biomarkers for liver disease in urea cycle disorders.
evidence.snippet: Increasingly, studies have demonstrated that children and adults with UCDs are also at risk for developing various forms of chronic liver disease

nodes.description: Hyperammonemic coma represents the most severe neurological presentation, occurring primarily in neonatal-onset forms. Prognosis is very poor when ammonia exceeds 1000 umol/L with prolonged coma.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: excessive ammonia leads to life-threatening conditions

nodes.description: This curated group entry focuses on loss of function in core urea-cycle enzymes required for nitrogen disposal.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: Congenital defects of the enzymes or transporters of the urea cycle cause the disease.

nodes.description: Acute or recurrent hyperammonemic encephalopathy with altered mental status, ranging from irritability and lethargy to coma. Severity is time- and concentration-dependent.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders in critically Ill adults.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia, particularly if severe, causes time- and concentration-dependent neurologic injury.

evidence.reference_title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: First revision.
evidence.snippet: UCDs cause hyperammonemia of neonatal (~50%) or late onset that can lead to intellectual disability or death

nodes.description: Poor growth is common due to protein-restricted diets, recurrent catabolic crises, and feeding difficulties.
Developmental delay is common in children with UCDs, particularly those with neonatal-onset disease or recurrent hyperammonemic episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: First revision.
evidence.snippet: UCDs cause hyperammonemia of neonatal (~50%) or late onset that can lead to intellectual disability or death

nodes.description: In argininosuccinic aciduria (ASL deficiency), glutathione biosynthesis is dysregulated with upregulated cysteine metabolism but depleted glutathione and downregulated antioxidant pathways. This redox defect provides a molecular basis for chronic liver disease manifestations in ASL deficiency beyond episodic hyperammonemia, and represents a target for mRNA therapy approaches.
nodes.id: Hepatic glutathione dysregulation in ASL deficiency

evidence.reference_title: mRNA therapy corrects defective glutathione metabolism and restores ureagenesis in preclinical argininosuccinic aciduria.
evidence.snippet: Here, we describe the dysregulation of glutathione biosynthesis and upstream cysteine utilization in ASL-deficient patients and mice

evidence.reference_title: mRNA therapy corrects defective glutathione metabolism and restores ureagenesis in preclinical argininosuccinic aciduria.
evidence.snippet: Human hASL mRNA encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles improved glutathione metabolism and chronic liver disease.

nodes.description: Liver enlargement can occur in UCDs, particularly in ASL deficiency where chronic liver disease with fibrosis may develop. Hepatic involvement is also seen in citrin deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Biomarkers for liver disease in urea cycle disorders.
evidence.snippet: The manifestations of chronic liver disease in UCDs are variable and may include elevated serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and/or aspartate aminotransferase (AST), hepatomegaly, abnormal grey-scale ultrasound pattern of the liver parenchyma, hepatic steatosis, hepatic fibrosis, cirrhosis with portal hypertension, and impaired liver function

nodes.description: Elevated plasma ammonia concentration is the defining clinical feature of UCDs. Ammonia rises especially during catabolic stress, infection, or protein loading. In neonates, levels >200 umol/L suggest IEM; after the neonatal period, >100 umol/L is concerning.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: The urea cycle disorders (UCDs) comprise diseases presenting with hyperammonemia that arise in either the neonatal period (about 50% of cases) or later.

evidence.reference_title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: First revision.
evidence.snippet: UCDs cause hyperammonemia of neonatal (~50%) or late onset that can lead to intellectual disability or death

nodes.description: Ammonia diffuses freely across the blood-brain barrier and is detoxified primarily in astrocytes by conversion to glutamine via glutamine synthetase. During hyperammonemic crises, glutamine becomes an osmolyte that drives astrocytic swelling and cytotoxic cerebral edema. This is accompanied by disrupted glutamate-glutamine cycling, excitotoxicity, impaired energy metabolism, and altered aquaporin-4 expression, culminating in encephalopathy and seizure susceptibility.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: The strategy for therapy is to prevent the irreversible toxicity of high-ammonia exposure to the brain.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders in adult patients: a tightrope walk between evidence-based medicine and expert opinion-a case series and systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Ammonia passes into the circulation and crosses the blood–brain barrier. The ammonia will exert a direct toxic effect on the neurotransmission responsible for part of the neurological symptomatology.

nodes.description: The urea cycle is the main pathway for ammonia detoxification, localized to periportal hepatocytes. Reduced conversion of ammonia to urea causes accumulation of circulating ammonia and nitrogenous intermediates. Proximal mitochondrial defects (CPS1, OTC, NAGS) produce hyperammonemia with low citrulline and arginine, while distal cytosolic defects (ASS1, ASL, ARG1) cause accumulation of pathway intermediates with variable hyperammonemia.
nodes.id: Impaired hepatic ureagenesis and ammonia accumulation

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: The urea cycle is a metabolic pathway for the disposal of excess nitrogen, which arises primarily as ammonia.

nodes.description: Long-term neurodevelopmental impairment is common in survivors of neonatal hyperammonemic crises and in those with recurrent episodes. Outcome correlates with peak ammonia level and duration of exposure.

evidence.reference_title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: First revision.
evidence.snippet: UCDs cause hyperammonemia of neonatal (~50%) or late onset that can lead to intellectual disability or death

nodes.description: Decreased alertness and feeding difficulty during acute metabolic decompensation. Often the earliest clinical sign of hyperammonemic crisis in neonates.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders in critically Ill adults.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia, particularly if severe, causes time- and concentration-dependent neurologic injury.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation remains the only curative option for severe UCDs, restoring full urea cycle capacity. It is indicated for patients with recurrent severe hyperammonemic crises despite maximal medical therapy. Benefits include elimination of hyperammonemia risk, dietary liberalization, and improved quality of life, but carry lifelong immunosuppression requirements.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term correction of urea cycle disorders.
evidence.snippet: Currently, orthotopic liver transplantation is the primary means of achieving this correction.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: N-acetylglutamate synthase and at least two transporter proteins are essential to urea cycle function.

nodes.description: Some UCDs are detectable through expanded newborn screening by tandem mass spectrometry. Citrullinemia type I (elevated citrulline) and ASL deficiency (elevated argininosuccinic acid) are most reliably detected. Early identification enables pre-symptomatic treatment initiation and prevention of neonatal crises.

evidence.reference_title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: First revision.
evidence.snippet: experience on newborn screening for some UCDs has widened

nodes.description: ASL participates in the citrulline-argininosuccinate-arginine recycling pathway that channels arginine to nitric oxide synthase. ASL deficiency impairs endogenous nitric oxide production, contributing to systemic vascular and neurological phenotypes that are independent of hyperammonemia, including hypertension and neurocognitive deficits.
nodes.id: Nitric oxide deficiency in ASL deficiency

evidence.reference_title: Requirement of argininosuccinate lyase for systemic nitric oxide production.
evidence.snippet:
Loss of Asl in both humans and mice leads to reduced NO
synthesis, owing to both decreased endogenous arginine synthesis and an impaired
ability to use extracellular arginine for NO production.

evidence.reference_title: Argininosuccinate lyase deficiency causes blood-brain barrier disruption via nitric oxide-mediated dysregulation of claudin expression.
evidence.snippet:
Previously, we have shown that 
argininosuccinate lyase deficiency (ASLD) is a novel model system to investigate 
cell-autonomous, nitric oxide synthase-dependent NO deficiency.

nodes.description: Sodium phenylbutyrate is metabolized to phenylacetate, which conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine, providing an alternative renal route for waste nitrogen excretion. Glycerol phenylbutyrate is a newer palatable formulation. Sodium benzoate conjugates with glycine to form hippurate for renal excretion.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: We review here the current concepts of the pathogenesis, diagnostics, including genetics and treatment of UCDs.

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders-update.
evidence.snippet: ornithine transcarbamylase are present in the mitochondrial matrix

evidence.reference_title: Urea cycle disorders in critically Ill adults.
evidence.snippet: Urea cycle disorders (UCDs) cause elevations in ammonia which, when severe, cause irreversible neurologic injury.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the Link: Seizure Characteristics and Ammonia Levels in Urea Cycle Disorder During Hyperammonemic Crises.
evidence.snippet: 1.14 (95% CI, 1.04 to 1.25) times for every 100 μmol/L increase in glutamine

nodes.description: Controlled protein intake to reduce nitrogen load while preserving growth is the cornerstone of chronic UCD management. Natural protein is limited and supplemented with essential amino acid mixtures to maintain adequate nutrition and growth.

evidence.reference_title: The nutritional management of urea cycle disorders.
evidence.snippet: Diet is one of the mainstays of the treatment of patients with urea cycle disorders.

nodes.description: Hyperventilation-driven respiratory alkalosis can be an early sign of hyperammonemia, as ammonia stimulates the central respiratory center. This may be the initial metabolic finding before frank metabolic crisis.

Seizures occur in approximately 13% of hyperammonemic events. Subclinical seizures are detected in 27% of encephalopathic crises without clinical seizures and 53% when clinical seizures are present. Seizure risk increases 2.65-fold per 100 umol/L ammonia increase.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the Link: Seizure Characteristics and Ammonia Levels in Urea Cycle Disorder During Hyperammonemic Crises.
evidence.snippet: Seizures are observed in 13% of HA events. Among all HA events with concomitant EEG, subclinical seizures were identified in 27% of crises of encephalopathy without clinical seizures and 53% of crises with clinical seizures.

nodes.description: Progressive spasticity, particularly spastic diplegia, is the predominant neurological phenotype in arginase deficiency (ARG1). This distinct presentation can lead to diagnostic confusion with cerebral palsy.
Recurrent vomiting with poor feeding during metabolic instability is a common presenting symptom, particularly in late-onset forms.
